4.2.title: Cloud Computing
4.2.content:
Pattern: You picked PaaS on 4 separate DaaS questions. PaaS is your "when in doubt" answer — it's almost never correct. DaaS (Desktop as a Service) is invisible to you — it's the answer whenever a question mentions virtual desktops, remote employees, per-user license, or "gaming PC in cloud."

4 Cloud Service Models

ModelWhat you getKeyword triggers
IaaSServers, storage, networking"servers and switches", "data center replacement", "quickly provisioned"
PaaSDevelopment platform"development environment", "deploy applications" (rarely the answer in your misses)
SaaSFinished apps"accounting software", "email service", "ready-to-use"
DaaSVirtual desktops in the cloud"virtual desktops", "remote employees", "per-user license", "gaming PC in cloud"

4 Cloud Deployment Models

ModelWhen to pick
PublicPay-as-you-go, shared, cost matters more than control
PrivateSensitive data, compliance, dedicated resources
HybridMix: sensitive on-prem + scale to public
CommunityRelated orgs share (gov, health)

Memory hook — "IP-SD": IaaS = Infrastructure · PaaS = Platform · SaaS = Software · DaaS = Desktops

4.1.title: Virtualization
4.1.content:
Pattern: Shadow PC / gaming-PC-in-cloud = DaaS (same trap as 4.2). VDI primary benefit = security/control, NOT "simplifies install" or "reduces network dependency." Type 1 hypervisor = BARE METAL (no host OS).

Key distinctions

  • Type 1 (bare-metal): Installs directly on hardware. No host OS. Enterprise/datacenter. ESXi, Hyper-V Server.
  • Type 2 (hosted): Runs ON TOP of an OS. Desktop use. VirtualBox, VMware Workstation.
  • VDI primary benefit: Desktop security and control (centralized policies/updates). NOT faster local processing, NOT reduced network dependency.
  • VM snapshot: Revert to pre-patch snapshot to fix bad patches in sandbox (don't "factory reset" or "disable sandbox").
  • Virtualized storage — scalable: NAS (not local SSD, not DAS, not USB).

Shadow PC trap: Any question describing "virtualized Windows PC in the cloud I access remotely" → DaaS. Even if it says "PC" or "gaming" in the name. Service model is DaaS.

5.5.title: Network Troubleshooting
5.5.content:
Pattern: Jitter vs latency vs throughput vs bandwidth confusion. Jumping to the wrong device when diagnosing. Misidentifying root cause.

Network performance metrics — drill these

MetricDefinitionTelltale symptom
LatencyRound-trip time for a packetSlow response, delay
JitterVARIATION in latency between packetsVoIP sounds like speeding up/slowing down. Video stutter.
ThroughputACTUAL transfer rate achievedLower than expected speed
BandwidthMAX THEORETICAL transfer rateWhat you pay your ISP for
Packet loss% of packets lostDropped calls, missing data

Jitter mnemonic: "Speeds up and slows down" + "VoIP / video conference" = JITTER. Latency = constant delay. Jitter = delay that VARIES.

Network diagnosis rules

  • Switch continually re-assigns a MAC between ports (CAM-table churn, aka MAC flapping) → duplicate MAC address, not collisions or port flapping
  • Devices on different VLANs can't communicate → need a router (or L3 switch). Hub won't work.
  • Can ping local workstations but not gateway → the router is down. Not DNS.
  • Can't reach by domain name BUT CAN by IP → DNS issue (port 53).
  • Microwave/fixed wireless intermittent after install → line of sight (trees, weather). Not split horizon.
  • Something was changed (replaced AP, new cable) → start troubleshooting AT THE THING THAT CHANGED.
  • WAP dropping connections after install → check placement / interference first (site survey). Not bandwidth saturation.
  • Speed dropped to 100 Mbps after Windows update → NIC driver reverted to generic. Roll back the driver.

3.5.title: Motherboards / CPU
3.5.content:
Pattern: 4 of 8 P1-P4 misses were PBQ motherboard-identification. Visual recognition is your biggest PBQ risk. mITX is 6.7"×6.7"; mATX is 9.6"×9.6". BIOS password vs Secure Boot — different things.

Motherboard form factors — MEMORIZE

Form factorSizeUsed for
ATX12" × 9.6"Full-size desktops, gaming
microATX (mATX)9.6" × 9.6"Smaller desktops
Mini-ITX (mITX)6.7" × 6.7"HTPCs, SFF, passive cooling, quiet

HTPC hook: "Home theater", "quiet", "small form factor", "living room" → mITX.

PBQ components — must recognize by sight

  • 24-pin mainboard power (top edge, big connector)
  • 4-pin fan power (small, near CPU and corners)
  • PCIe slots — x1 (shortest) → x4 → x8 → x16 (longest, for GPU)
  • DIMM / RAM slots (long, parallel to top edge, 2-4 of them)
  • SATA connectors (small L-shaped, at edge)
  • M.2 slot (small horizontal slot near PCIe)
  • CPU socket (square, large, center)
  • Front-panel header (tiny pins cluster at bottom edge — power/LED/reset)
  • CMOS battery (round silver coin cell)

BIOS vs Secure Boot vs TPM — don't confuse

FeatureWhat it doesWhen it's the answer
BIOS passwordLocks BIOS settings"Prevent changing boot order", "lock BIOS", "prevent booting from USB"
Boot passwordRequired to boot system"Password before OS loads"
Secure BootVerifies OS kernel signatures"Prevent unauthorized/rogue OS from loading"
TPMHardware crypto keys"Hardware key storage", BitLocker

Other 3.5 gotchas: Password cracking / crypto mining → dedicated GPU (not multi-core CPU). Parallel math is way faster on GPU.

3.2.title: Cables & Connectors
3.2.content:
Pattern: Visual PBQ port ID + CAT cable over-picking (CAT 6 when 5e is cheapest gigabit). USB port colors: BLACK = 2.0, BLUE/RED = 3.0+.

USB standards & port colors

StandardSpeedPort color
USB 1.112 MbpsWhite/black
USB 2.0480 MbpsBLACK
USB 3.0 / 3.1 Gen 15 GbpsBLUE
USB 3.1 Gen 210 GbpsTeal/red
USB 3.2 Gen 2×220 GbpsRed
USB 4 / Thunderbolt 440 GbpsUSB-C (no color)

Ethernet CAT — speed + cost

CableSpeedCost note
CAT 5100 MbpsObsolete
CAT 5e1 GbpsCheapest gigabit-capable. Default.
CAT 61 Gbps full / 10 Gbps up to 55mMore expensive than 5e
CAT 6a10 Gbps full 100mFor 10 Gbps at full distance
CAT 710 GbpsProprietary shielded
CAT 825-40 Gbps (short)Datacenter only

CAT trap: 750 Mbps at lowest price → CAT 5e (not 6). Always pick the cheapest cable that meets the spec. For 10 Gbps at 100m → CAT 6a.

Video connector identification

ConnectorHow to spot
VGABlue, 15-pin, 3 rows
DVI-DDigital only, DASH only (no cross)
DVI-IHas the CROSS (4 pins around dash) — analog + digital
HDMITrapezoidal, small, common
DisplayPortLike HDMI but one corner cut
ThunderboltMini DisplayPort or USB-C

Power connectors

  • Molex (4-pin, white): DC power for HDD, CD/DVD
  • Mini-Molex (4-pin smaller): floppy drives only
  • SATA power (15-pin, flat): HDD/SSD
  • 24-pin ATX: motherboard main power
  • 4/8-pin EPS: CPU power (near CPU socket)

5.6.title: Printer Troubleshooting
5.6.content:
Pattern: You picked "printer share on network switch" on the SAME question 3 times. Correct is always "share via local host" when the printer has no RJ-45/WiFi. Laser prints 60-70% then fails = OUT OF MEMORY.

Must-memorize scenarios

ScenarioCorrect answer
Share printer with no RJ-45/WiFi (simplest)Share from local host (host stays on)
Budget upgradeWireless print server OR network printer
Laser prints 60-70% of photo then failsOut of memory (needs more RAM)
USB printer won't print, "nothing changed"Establish a theory (step 2) — DON'T unplug/reboot
Vector prints OK but bitmap failsRAM limitation

Printer type maintenance

  • Laser: toner, drum, fuser, transfer belt. Replace toner, apply maintenance kit, calibrate, clean.
  • Inkjet: cartridges, printhead, rollers, feeder. Clean printheads, replace cartridges, calibrate, clear jams.
  • Thermal: feed assembly, heating element, special paper. Replace paper, clean element.
  • Impact: ribbon, printhead, multipart paper. Replace all three.

2.2.title: Wireless Networking
2.2.content:
Pattern: 54 Mbps on 2.4 GHz = 802.11g (not a or b). Z-Wave = 800-900 MHz proprietary. RFID = one-way; NFC = two-way, 4cm. Indoor location = Bluetooth (not RFID).

802.11 standards — memorize cold

StandardAlso calledFrequencyMax speed
802.11aWireless A5 GHz54 Mbps
802.11bWireless B2.4 GHz11 Mbps
802.11gWireless G2.4 GHz54 Mbps
802.11nWi-Fi 42.4 / 5 GHzUp to 600 Mbps
802.11acWi-Fi 55 GHz onlyUp to 3.5 Gbps
802.11axWi-Fi 6 / 6E2.4 / 5 / 6 GHzUp to 9.6 Gbps
802.11beWi-Fi 72.4 / 5 / 6 GHz46 Gbps

Memory hook: A = 5 GHz alone. B, G, N = 2.4 GHz (N is also 5). 54 Mbps appears for both A and G — difference is frequency. AC is ALWAYS 5 GHz only. AX adds 2.4 + 5 + 6.

5 GHz vs 2.4 GHz trade-offs

2.4 GHz5 GHz
RangeLONGERShorter
Wall penetrationBetterWorse
Power consumptionLowerHigher
Channels availableFewer (congested)More non-overlapping
InterferenceMore (microwaves, BT)Less
SpeedLowerHigher

Home automation + RFID/NFC

TechFrequencyDirectionUse case
Z-Wave800-900 MHz (proprietary)MeshHome automation
Zigbee2.4 GHz (open source)MeshHome automation, IoT
RFIDVariousONE-WAYInventory tags, asset tracking
NFC13.56 MHz (~4cm)TWO-WAYContactless payment
Bluetooth indoor location2.4 GHz—3-4m indoor positioning

3.1.title: Display Components
3.1.content:
Pattern: LCD with fluorescent backlight = needs INVERTER (not plasma). Refresh rate vs frame rate are different. TN = gaming (high Hz); IPS = video editing (color accuracy). OLED = no backlight.

Display technology distinctions

TechKey featureBest for
LCD (TFT) + CCFLNeeds INVERTER (AC for fluorescent backlight)Legacy laptops
LCD + LED backlightNo inverter (DC LEDs)Modern laptops
TN panelFast response, HIGH refresh (120/144 Hz), poor colorGAMING
IPS panelGreat color accuracy, wide viewing angles, typically 60 HzVIDEO EDITING, design
OLEDSelf-emissive (NO backlight), deep blacks, thin/lightReplace CRT, premium
PlasmaHot, not energy efficient, obsoleteRarely the answer

Rate terminology

TermDefinition
Refresh rateHow many times/sec the MONITOR redraws (Hz). Fixed by hardware.
Frame rate (FPS)How many frames/sec the GPU sends. Variable by software.
Response timeHow fast a pixel changes color (ms). Lower = less ghosting.
Aspect ratioWidth:height
ResolutionTotal pixels

1.1.title: Mobile Device Hardware
1.1.content:
Pattern: Wireless 802.11ac card in laptop = Mini PCIe (not SODIMM — that's RAM). Webcam detected in BIOS but not OS → physical connection issue, not software. Conference room mic = omnidirectional.

Laptop expansion card types

Form factorWhat it is
SODIMMLaptop RAM. NOT an expansion card for networking.
Mini PCIe (mPCIe)Laptop wireless / cellular cards
M.2Modern successor — supports WiFi, SSD, cellular
NVMe (via M.2 or U.2)Flash storage interface

Microphone types

PatternPicks up fromUse case
OmnidirectionalAll directions (360°)Conference rooms, multi-speaker
CardioidFront primarily (heart shape)Podcasts, vocals
UnidirectionalOne directionInterview
BidirectionalFront + back (figure 8)Two-person face-to-face

5 authentication factors (CompTIA loves this)

FactorExamples
Something you KNOWPassword, PIN, security question
Something you HAVESmart card, token, phone (SMS)
Something you AREFingerprint, retina, facial (biometric)
Something you DOSignature, keystroke dynamics
Somewhere you AREGPS location, IP geofence

2.1.title: TCP/UDP Ports & Protocols
2.1.content:
Pattern: Your most persistent gap. Missed 6x across 5 pretests. Drill these.

Port numbers to memorize

PortProtocol
20, 21FTP
22SSH / SFTP
23Telnet
25SMTP
53DNS
67, 68DHCP
69TFTP
80HTTP
110POP3
137, 139NetBIOS/NetBT
143IMAP
161SNMP
443HTTPS
445SMB (NOT 139)
3389RDP

Common traps

  • FTP = 20/21, SFTP = 22 (not 20/21 for both)
  • Port 139 = NetBIOS, not SMB. SMB = 445.
  • Can access by IP but NOT by domain name → DNS blocked → unblock port 53.
  • TFTP for VoIP phone boot config = port 69 (not 21).

5.2.title: Drive & Boot Troubleshooting
5.2.content:
• PXE booting instead of HDD → enter BIOS, change boot order to HDD. Recovery mode won't help. Rebooting won't help (PXE again).
  • BOOTMGR missing error → boot from install disc → "Repair your computer" → CLI → bootrec /fixmbr.
  • HDD no longer recognized by UEFI (cables verified OK) → hard drive has failed. Wrong boot order would still show the drive.
  • Accident-prone customer drops laptop, platter fails repeatedly → replace with SSD or NVMe (no moving parts). Hybrid still has platters.
  • Compare SSD vs HDD performance → IOPS. Seek time only applies to HDD.
  • RAID levels: 0 = striping (no redundancy). 1 = mirror. 5 = distributed parity (min 3 drives). 6 = double parity (min 4). 10 = mirrored stripes (min 4). RAID 10 with four 10TB = 20TB usable.

5.1.title: Motherboard / RAM / CPU / Power Troubleshooting
5.1.content:
• Grinding noise from PSU fan → replace the entire PSU. Never repair a PSU (high voltage).
  • System clock keeps resetting → CMOS battery. Not CPU, not RAM, not PSU.
  • Foul chemical smell (not burning) → motherboard (distended capacitor). Burning smell → PSU first.
  • Overheating → check fans, thermal paste, dust, airflow.

5.4.title: Mobile Device Troubleshooting
5.4.content:
• iPhone force-restart: power + volume DOWN (not UP). Missed this twice.
  • Laptop won't power on + battery replaced + different adapter tried → DC jack is faulty.
  • Water-damaged laptop, first steps to examine components → organize screws on a grid/diagram on paper before disassembly. Not rice.

2.7.title: Internet Connection Types
2.7.content:
TypeKey fact
CableHFC (hybrid fiber-coaxial), uses cable modem
DSLTwisted-pair copper (phone lines)
Fiber (FTTH)Uses ONT (optical network terminal)
SatelliteHigh latency, rural/remote
Cellular (fixed wireless)Licensed — needs FCC license
Long-range wireless (unlicensed)Line-of-sight microwave, 802.11 directional, under FCC power limits (~200 mW)
  • LAN = local only. WAN = spans geography (internet = WAN).
  • PAN = personal/Bluetooth range. MAN = metro/city area (municipal Wi-Fi mesh ~5 square blocks).

2.4.title: Network Configuration
2.4.content:
DNS records

RecordWhat it does
AHostname → IPv4
AAAAHostname → IPv6
CNAMEAlias one name to another
MXMail exchange
TXTHuman-readable, domain verification (SPF/DKIM/DMARC)
NSAuthoritative DNS server for a domain
SRVService info (VoIP, IM)
PTRReverse DNS
  • VLAN = logical separation over same physical switch. Different VLANs need a router to communicate.
  • DHCP reservation = assigns a specific IP based on MAC address.

1.3.title: Mobile Network Connectivity
1.3.content:
• 4G = LTE + LTE-A. LTE theoretical 150 Mbps / real ~20. LTE-A theoretical 300 / real ~40.
  • 5G = low/mid/high-band mmWave. High-band theoretical up to 70 Gbps.
  • 3G = HSPA+ and EV-DO.
  • Cellular unavailable (airplane) → enable Wi-Fi. Not Bluetooth, not GPS.
  • Trackpad cursor too fast → Settings > Devices > Touchpad > "Change the cursor speed" slider. NOT sensitivity (that's click pressure).

1.2.title: Mobile Device Accessories
1.2.content:
• Track point = pressure-sensitive nub between keys (ThinkPad-style red nub).
  • Trackpad = flat touch surface below keyboard.
  • Stylus = pen for touchscreens.

2.6.title: Network Addressing (Public vs Private IP)
2.6.content:
Private IP ranges (RFC 1918)

RangeType
10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255Private
172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255Private
192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255Private
169.254.x.xAPIPA (DHCP failure)
127.0.0.1Localhost/loopback

If an IP is NOT in any of the above ranges → it's public.

3.6.title: Power Supplies
3.6.content:
• Traveling worldwide / international → dual-voltage PSU (120V / 240V).
  • 120V PSU in a 240V outlet → destroyed.
  • 240V PSU in a 120V outlet → won't function.
  • Wattage and 12V rail amperage matter, but input voltage is the key consideration for international use.

2.5.title: Networking Hardware
2.5.content:
• HFC (hybrid fiber-coaxial) broadband → cable modem.
  • Fiber to home → ONT (optical network terminal).
  • Proactively stop outside attackers → IPS (not IDS — IDS only logs; not proxy).
  • Firewall + malware scanner + multiple security functions combined → UTM (Unified Threat Management).

3.4.title: Storage Devices
3.4.content:
• NAS essentials: media streaming, file sharing, Gigabit NIC, RAID array, multiple HDDs.
  • RAID 5 = speed + redundancy. RAID 0 = speed only.
  • RAID 10 (4× 10TB) → 20TB usable (half used by mirroring).
  • Gigabit NIC > FastEthernet (100 Mbps).
  • For NAS: HDDs > SSDs (more storage, lower cost per TB).

3.7.title: Printer Deployment
3.7.content:
• Small office + 4 PCs + no open switch ports + no servers → connect printer to one workstation, share it. That workstation must stay on.
  • Collate = pages in order (1,2,3,1,2,3). Not duplex.
  • Duplex = prints on both sides.
  • Mac prints fail to network printer, Windows works → install macOS (OS X) drivers on the print server.

troubleshooting.title: CompTIA 6-Step Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.content:
You missed this across multiple OBJs. Memorize the ORDER.

#StepWhat it means
1Identify the problemGather info. Question the user. BACK UP DATA. Identify recent changes.
2Establish a theory of probable causeQuestion the obvious. Most likely cause. Research if needed.
3Test the theoryConfirmed → move to plan. Disproven → new theory OR escalate.
4Establish plan + implementDocument the plan. Execute.
5Verify full system functionalityConfirm fix works. Preventive measures.
6Document findings, actions, outcomesWrite it down. Every time.

Why you miss this: Default is to jump to step 4 (unplug, reboot, reinstall). The exam punishes this. "What do you do NEXT" after identifying the problem → answer is usually "establish a theory" (step 2), NOT take action.

Mnemonic that actually lines up — first letter of each word = first letter of each step (I · E · T · E · V · D):

Mnemonic wordStep
Identify1. Identify the problem
Engineers2. Establish a theory
Test3. Test the theory
Everything4. Establish a plan + implement
Very5. Verify full system functionality
Deliberately6. Document findings

"Identify, Engineers Test Everything Very Deliberately." If that doesn't stick, just remember the letters: I E T E V D.
